verses.flow: The words of Qohelet (Qoheleth), the son of David, king in Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qohelet" (Strong's H6953) derives from the root qahal, meaning "to assemble" or "to gather a congregation." The form is a feminine participial noun, likely a title or office meaning "one who assembles" or "the Assembler/Preacher." The use of a title rather than a personal name gives the book a universal, representative quality. The superscription identifies him as a son of David and king in Jerusalem, pointing to Solomonic authorship in the tradition.

verses.flow: Vanity (hebel) of vanities, says Qohelet, vanity of vanities! All is vanity.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hebel" (Strong's H1892) literally means "breath" or "vapor" — something insubstantial, fleeting, and without enduring weight. The superlative construction "hebel of hebels" (hebel habalim) is the Hebrew idiom for the absolute extreme, as in "Song of Songs" or "Holy of Holies." The word appears approximately 38 times in Ecclesiastes, forming the structural spine of the entire book. It does not mean "meaningless" in a nihilistic sense but rather "transient" and "elusive."

verses.flow: What profit (yitron) is there to a man in all his toil in which he toils under the sun?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yitron" (Strong's H3504) is a commercial term meaning "surplus," "profit," or "advantage" — what remains after accounts are settled. It appears only in Ecclesiastes in the Hebrew Bible, suggesting a deliberately mercantile metaphor for evaluating human endeavor. "Under the sun" (tachat hashemesh) is the book's signature phrase, appearing 29 times, demarcating the earthly, mortal sphere of inquiry apart from divine perspective. THEOLOGICAL: The question is rhetorical and sets the epistemological framework: from the horizontal plane of human observation alone, no ultimate surplus can be demonstrated.

verses.flow: A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth stands forever (le'olam).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between the passing of generations and the abiding earth introduces the theme of cosmic permanence versus human transience. The earth's endurance is not celebrated but serves as a foil to human frailty. This verse anticipates the New Testament's reconfiguration: ultimately the earth itself will pass away (Matt 5:18; Rev 21:1), and only that which is in Christ endures.

verses.flow: That which has been is that which will be, and that which has been done is that which will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the philosophical climax of the opening reflection, asserting the cyclical and non-progressive nature of history as observed from below the sun. It does not deny divine novelty or eschatological newness — the prophets and the New Testament speak of a "new thing" YHWH will do (Isa 43:19; Rev 21:5) — but affirms that within the closed system of fallen creation, genuine novelty is inaccessible to human perception. TYPOLOGY: The "new creation" in Christ (2 Cor 5:17) is precisely the answer to this declaration.

verses.flow: And I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom (chokmah) concerning all that is done under the heavens. It is an evil task (inyan ra) that God has given to the sons of man to be afflicted with.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokmah" (Strong's H2451) refers to practical and intellectual skill, the faculty of discernment applied to the nature of reality. "Inyan ra" literally means "an evil business" or "a wretched occupation" — a startlingly severe verdict on the project of human wisdom-seeking. "Sons of man" (bene ha'adam) echoes Genesis 1-2, grounding the inquiry in creaturely limitation. THEOLOGICAL: God has given this afflictive task not as punishment per se but as the creaturely condition that drives the wise toward the fear of YHWH rather than self-sufficient rationalism.

verses.flow: I have seen all the works that are done under the sun, and behold, all is vanity and a striving after wind (re'ut ruach).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Re'ut ruach" (or in some manuscripts "ra'yon ruach") means literally "a feeding on wind" or "a shepherding of wind" — an image of utter futility, grasping at what cannot be held. The Septuagint renders it "proairesis pneumatos" (choice of spirit/wind). Some scholars connect ruach here to the Aramaic cognate meaning "desire" or "will," yielding "a chasing after desire." Either reading intensifies the futility: wind cannot be caught, and desire cannot be permanently satisfied.

verses.flow: That which is crooked cannot be made straight, and that which is lacking cannot be numbered.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This aphorism functions as a limit-statement on human wisdom: creation as it exists in its fallen, disordered state contains distortions that unaided human effort cannot rectify. The "crooked" (me'uvvat) may allude to the moral and structural disorder introduced by the fall. TYPOLOGY: The Servant of YHWH makes crooked places straight (Isa 40:4; 45:2), and the Messiah accomplishes the rectification that Qohelet declares humanly impossible (Luke 3:5).

verses.flow: For in much wisdom is much vexation (ka'as), and he who increases knowledge increases sorrow (mak'ob).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ka'as" (Strong's H3708) means grief, vexation, or provocation — the emotional distress that accompanies unsatisfied inquiry. "Mak'ob" (Strong's H4341) means pain or sorrow, used of deep suffering (cf. Isa 53:3, where the Suffering Servant is described as acquainted with mak'ob). TYPOLOGY: The juxtaposition of wisdom and sorrow here finds its ultimate resolution in Christ, who bore the fullness of human mak'ob (Isa 53:3-4) and in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Col 2:3).

verses.flow: I said in my heart, "Come now, I will test you with pleasure (simchah); enjoy good things." And behold, this also was vanity (hebel).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Vanity" translates hebel (Strong's H1892), literally "breath" or "vapor" — a word denoting that which is insubstantial, fleeting, and without lasting substance. It is the governing term of the entire book (appearing ~38 times), introduced here as the interpretive lens through which all human experience under the sun is evaluated. "Pleasure" is simchah (H8057), denoting joy or gladness, often in festive contexts.

verses.flow: Of laughter I said, "It is madness (mehollal)," and of pleasure (simchah), "What does this accomplish?"

verses.flow: I searched in my heart how to cheer my flesh with wine — my heart still guiding me with wisdom — and how to lay hold on folly, until I might see what is good for the children of man to do under the heavens during the few days of their life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Qohelet's experiment is not mere hedonism but a deliberate, wisdom-guided investigation into human flourishing. The phrase "my heart still guiding me with wisdom" is crucial — he pursues pleasure not as a fool but as a sage seeking to determine whether pleasure holds ultimate meaning. The phrase "under the heavens" (tachat hashamayim) is a rare variant of the more common "under the sun," suggesting the whole creaturely realm.

verses.flow: I made great my works: I built for myself houses, I planted for myself vineyards.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: I made for myself gardens and parks (pardesim), and I planted in them every kind of fruit tree.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Parks" translates pardesim (H6508), a Persian loanword (Old Persian: pairi-daeza, meaning "walled enclosure") from which the Greek paradeisos and English "paradise" derive. The presence of this Persian loanword is noted by some scholars as evidence of the book's post-exilic composition. Royal gardens were significant symbols of power and cosmic order in ancient Near Eastern ideology, as seen in Assyrian and Babylonian palace reliefs.

verses.flow: I made for myself pools of water, to water from them a forest (ya'ar) of growing trees.

verses.flow: I gathered for myself also silver and gold and the treasure of kings and of the provinces. I obtained for myself male singers and female singers, and the delights of the sons of man — concubines (shiddah v'shiddot) many.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Concubines" renders the obscure Hebrew shiddah v'shiddot (H7705), one of the most debated hapax legomena in the Hebrew Bible. The LXX translates it as "cup-bearers," while the Targum and Talmud (Gittin 68a) interpret it as referring to female demons or concubines. Most modern scholars follow the sense of "many concubines" based on context. The accumulation of treasure and singers recalls the grandeur of the Solomonic court (1 Kgs 10:14–29).

"Advantage" translates yitron (H3504), a commercial term for profit or surplus unique to Ecclesiastes in the Hebrew Bible, underscoring the book's economic metaphor for human endeavor.

verses.flow: The wise man — his eyes are in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I myself perceived that one fate (miqreh) overtakes them all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The concept of miqreh (H4745), "fate" or "chance-occurrence," is theologically significant. Qohelet does not use this term to deny divine providence but to assert the empirical reality that death comes to all — wise and fool alike — as observed from "under the sun." This horizontal observation is balanced later by the vertical perspective (12:14) that God brings every deed into judgment. The tension is intentional, not contradictory.

verses.flow: Then I said in my heart, "As the fate of the fool, so it will overtake me also. Why then have I been so exceedingly wise?" And I said in my heart that this also is vanity (hebel).
For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise, just as of the fool — since in the days that are coming all will have already been forgotten. And how does the wise man die? Just like the fool!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The theme of the erasure of memory is central to Qohelet's argument. In the ANE, to be remembered was to continue existing in a meaningful sense; to be forgotten was a form of final death. Qohelet's observation that even the wise are forgotten undercuts one of the primary motivations for virtuous achievement. This creates the existential crisis that the fear of God alone can resolve (12:13–14), and points forward to the resurrection hope that conquers the oblivion of Sheol (Dan 12:2).

verses.flow: There is nothing better for a man than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the first of seven "commendation of joy" passages in Ecclesiastes (also 3:12–13, 3:22, 5:18–20, 8:15, 9:7–9, 11:9–10). Rather than cynical resignation, these passages represent a positive theological conclusion: creaturely enjoyment received as gift from the hand of God is itself a form of wisdom. The phrase "from the hand of God" (miyyad ha'elohim) signals that true enjoyment is not self-generated but divinely bestowed, anticipating the New Testament theology of reception and gratitude (1 Tim 4:4; Jas 1:17).

verses.flow: For to the man who is good (tov) before him, God gives wisdom and knowledge and joy (simchah), but to the sinner he gives the task of gathering and heaping up, only to give to one who is good before God. This also is vanity (hebel) and a striving after wind (re'ut ruach).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This concluding verse introduces a moral framework that qualifies the entire chapter's experiment. The "good before him" (tov lefanav) designates one who stands in right covenantal relationship with God. The irony that the sinner's labor ultimately accrues to the righteous echoes Proverbs 13:22 and points to the eschatological reversal motif present throughout Scripture (cf. Matt 25:29). "Vanity" here may carry a more specifically moral connotation — that the sinner's frantic accumulation is not only futile but spiritually disordered. TYPOLOGY: The one who is "good before God" and receives wisdom, knowledge, and joy as divine gift anticipates the Messiah himself, in whom "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Col 2:3), and who as the true and greater Solomon embodies the enjoyment and wisdom that Qohelet sought but could not fully secure.

verses.flow: For everything there is a season ('et), and a time for every matter under the heavens:
verses.note: HEBREW: The word 'et (Strong's H6256) means an appointed time or fixed season, distinct from chronological time. The catalogue of 28 polar opposites (14 pairs) reflects a Semitic literary device of merism — expressing totality through polar extremes — suggesting that divine sovereignty encompasses all of human experience. STRUCTURE: The number 14 (7x2) may be intentional, invoking the fullness of divine completeness.

verses.flow: A time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted;

verses.flow: A time to tear, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak;
A time to love, and a time to hate; a time for war, and a time for peace (shalom);
verses.note: HEBREW: Shalom (Strong's H7965) here stands as the culminating positive pole in the entire catalogue, contrasted with war. Its placement at the end of the 28-item list gives it rhetorical weight as the telos of the sequence. THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of love/hate with war/shalom reflects a covenantal framework — hesed and shalom are the goal of YHWH's purposes for his people, even amid the full spectrum of earthly experience.

verses.flow: He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has put eternity (ha'olam) into their heart, yet so that man cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.
verses.note: HEBREW: Ha'olam (Strong's H5769) is rendered here as "eternity" though it can also mean "the hidden thing" or "the age." The Masoretic pointing supports the sense of indefinite duration or the totality of time. The Septuagint reads aiona (age/eternity). THEOLOGICAL: This verse is theologically pivotal: God plants a longing for the infinite within finite creatures, yet withholds full comprehension — a deliberate limitation that keeps humanity in dependence and wonder before the Creator. TYPOLOGY: This "eternity in the heart" finds its resolution only in the Messiah, in whom all things are summed up (Eph 1:10), and who is the wisdom of God made manifest (1 Cor 1:30).

verses.flow: For the fate of the sons of man and the fate of the beast is the same fate; as the one dies, so dies the other. They all have one breath (ruach), and man has no advantage (mowtar) over the beast, for all is vanity (hebel).
verses.note: HEBREW: Hebel (Strong's H1892), the governing word of Ecclesiastes, literally means "breath" or "vapor" — something insubstantial and fleeting. The same word is used as the proper name Abel (Hevel) in Genesis 4, possibly evoking the archetype of human brevity. Ruach (Strong's H7307) means breath, wind, or spirit — its ambiguity here is deliberate. THEOLOGICAL: The Qohelet does not deny the afterlife but reflects on what is observable "under the sun" — the empirical equality of death for beast and human. This is not materialist reductionism but a rhetorical device to deepen the weight of mortality and divine judgment.

verses.flow: Who knows whether the ruach of the sons of man goes upward, and the ruach of the beast goes down to the earth?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question ("who knows?") does not deny resurrection but highlights the limits of natural observation. Within the canonical witness, this tension is resolved eschatologically in the resurrection of the Messiah (1 Cor 15:20-22), who is the firstfruits of those who have died, demonstrating that the ruach of man does indeed ascend. TYPOLOGY: The upward movement of the human ruach anticipates the New Testament doctrine of the resurrection and the Spirit's role as guarantor of eternal life (Rom 8:11).

verses.flow: So I saw that there is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his work, for that is his portion (cheleq); for who can bring him to see what will be after him?
verses.note: HEBREW: Cheleq (Strong's H2506) means "portion" or "lot" — one's allotted share. This term recurs throughout Ecclesiastes as a key concept: within the constraints of mortality and divine inscrutability, joyful engagement with one's present work is itself a divinely assigned portion. THEOLOGICAL: This is not Epicurean hedonism but covenantal realism — the enjoyment of present gifts within the framework of the fear of God and ultimate accountability (Eccl 12:13-14). The question "who can bring him to see what will be after him?" underscores that man's portion is the present, while the future belongs to YHWH.

verses.flow: And I turned, and I saw all the oppressions that are done under the sun. And behold, the tears of the oppressed, and they have no comforter; and on the side of their oppressors was power, and they have no comforter.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "oppressions" is 'ashuqim (H6217), from the root 'ashaq, meaning to press upon, defraud, or extort. The double mention of "no comforter" (ein menahhem, cf. Lam 1:9) is a deliberate rhetorical emphasis in the Hebrew, underscoring the utter abandonment of the oppressed. THEOLOGICAL: Qohelet's observation here stands in tension with covenant theology, for YHWH is the defender of the oppressed (Ps 146:7); the Teacher speaks from "under the sun" — a perspective bounded by mortal sight, not divine resolution.

verses.flow: And I saw all the toil and all the skill of work, that it is a man's envy of his neighbor. This also is vanity (hebel) and a striving after wind.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term translated "envy" is qin'ah (H7068), which can also mean "zeal" or "jealousy," suggesting that competitive striving, even when energetic, is rooted in a disordered desire to surpass one's neighbor. THEOLOGICAL: This verse anticipates New Testament warnings against selfish ambition (Gal 5:20; James 3:14-16), revealing that the cycle of labor driven by rivalry is spiritually empty regardless of its material success.

verses.flow: The fool folds his hands and eats his own flesh.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Folds his hands" is chobeq yadayim (H2263), an idiom for idleness (cf. Prov 6:10; 24:33). "Eats his own flesh" (okhel et-besaro) is a striking idiom meaning self-destruction through sloth. THEOLOGICAL: Verse 5 functions as a counterbalance to verse 6, preventing the praise of quietness from becoming an endorsement of laziness; the Teacher holds both truths in productive tension.

verses.flow: Better is a handful of quietness than two fistfuls of toil and striving after wind.

verses.flow: And I turned, and I saw vanity under the sun:

verses.flow: There is one who is alone and without a second; indeed, he has neither son nor brother, yet there is no end to all his toil, and his eyes are never satisfied with riches. And for whom am I toiling and depriving myself of good? This also is vanity and an unhappy business.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "unhappy business" is inyan ra (H6040 + H7451), literally "an evil occupation" or "a grievous affair." The solitary man's rhetorical question — "for whom am I toiling?" — is the existential crisis of a self that has no relational telos. THEOLOGICAL: This verse prefigures the New Testament theology of the Body (1 Cor 12), where isolated self-sufficiency is shown to be contrary to the created order of human community and covenant.

verses.flow: Two are better than one, for they have a good reward for their toil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The praise of companionship in vv. 9-12 grounds human sociality in practical wisdom rather than mere sentiment. TYPOLOGY: The threefold cord of v. 12 has been read in the Christian tradition as an image of covenantal community — husband, wife, and YHWH — though the primary referent is likely the military or contractual solidarity of two companions with a third guarantee.

verses.flow: For if they fall, the one will lift up his companion. But woe to him who is alone when he falls, for he has no second to lift him up.

verses.flow: Again, if two lie together, they keep warm, but how can one keep warm alone?

verses.flow: And if a man prevails against him who is alone, two shall withstand him; and a threefold cord is not quickly broken.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Threefold cord" is ha-chut ha-meshulaash (H2339 + H8027), a cord twisted of three strands, which in ancient Near Eastern rope-making was recognized as exponentially stronger than a single or double strand. HISTORICAL: Contracts and treaties in the ANE were sometimes witnessed by a third party whose presence gave the agreement binding force, lending this imagery covenantal overtones beyond mere practicality.

verses.flow: Better is a poor and wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knows how to receive warning.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "poor and wise youth" contrasted with the "old and foolish king" may reflect the real political dynamics of court succession in ancient Israel and the surrounding ANE, where wisdom literature often challenged the presumed authority of entrenched power with the virtue of practical wisdom. TYPOLOGY: The figure of the wise youth rising from poverty and imprisonment (v. 14) evokes the Joseph narrative (Gen 41:14) and ultimately the rejected-and-exalted motif fulfilled in the Messiah (Ps 118:22; Acts 4:11).

verses.flow: For from the house of the prisoners he came forth to reign, although in his own kingdom he had been born poor.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Rising "from the house of the prisoners" to reign is a precise echo of Joseph's story (Gen 41:14) and anticipates the pattern of humiliation-then-exaltation that defines messianic typology — the servant who is despised, imprisoned, and then enthroned (Isa 53; Phil 2:6-11). THEOLOGICAL: Qohelet does not resolve whether the youth's reign brings lasting justice; the Teacher's point is that even such a remarkable reversal of fortune is subject to the same vanity under the sun.

verses.flow: I saw all the living who move about under the sun, along with the second youth who was to stand in his place.
There was no end to all the people, to all before whom he was. Yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and a striving after wind.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The final note of this section — that even the celebrated successor is forgotten by those who come after — reinforces the book's central thesis that no human achievement, however remarkable, escapes the leveling force of time and forgetfulness. TYPOLOGY: Only the reign of the Messiah, whose name endures forever (Ps 72:17; Rev 11:15), breaks this cycle of forgotten kings — the answer "above the sun" to Qohelet's lament "under the sun."

verses.flow: Guard your feet when you go to the house of God. Draw near to hear rather than to offer the sacrifice of fools, for they do not know that they are doing evil.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse opens with "Guard your feet" (shamar regel), an idiom for careful, deliberate conduct. "House of God" (bet ha-Elohim) here refers to the Jerusalem Temple. The contrast drawn is between the "hearing" posture of the wise and the "sacrifice of fools" (zebach kesilim) — suggesting rote ritual divorced from moral attentiveness. THEOLOGICAL: The Qohelet here anticipates the prophetic critique of empty sacrifice (Isa 1:11-15; Amos 5:21-24), grounding worship in obedience and humility rather than outward performance.

verses.flow: Be not rash with your mouth, and let not your heart be hasty to utter a word before God, for God is in the heavens and you are upon the earth; therefore let your words be few.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rash" (bahal, H926) carries the sense of being startled or thrown into haste. The theological premise is spatial and ontological: God's transcendence ("in the heavens") over human finitude ("upon the earth") is the basis for verbal restraint. THEOLOGICAL: This verse anticipates the apophatic tradition — the recognition that human language is inherently limited before divine majesty. Compare Job 40:4-5 and Hab 2:20.

THEOLOGICAL: The integrity of speech before God is a covenantal concern; broken vows profane the Name. Compare Deut 23:21-23 and Matt 5:33-37.

verses.flow: Do not allow your mouth to bring your flesh into guilt, and do not say before the messenger (mal'ak) that it was an error. Why should God be angry at your voice and destroy the work of your hands?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Messenger" (mal'ak, H4397) is debated — likely referring to the priestly representative (cf. Mal 2:7, where the priest is called the "messenger of YHWH of Hosts") before whom vows were to be paid. The term could also denote an angelic figure, given its dual usage in Hebrew. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The possible angelic referent reflects the broader Second Temple understanding that heavenly beings oversee cultic fidelity, an awareness consistent with divine council theology (cf. 1 Cor 11:10).

verses.flow: For in the multitude of dreams and vanities (hebel) there are also many words; but fear God.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hebel" (H1892), the key term of Ecclesiastes meaning "vapor," "breath," or "futility," appears here in the context of dreams and many words. The verse closes with an abrupt imperative: "fear God" (yare' et-ha-Elohim), functioning as a theological anchor amid the surrounding vanities. THEOLOGICAL: The fear of God (yir'at Elohim) is the structural frame of the entire book (cf. 1:3; 12:13-14) and represents the only stable orientation in a world of hebel.

verses.flow: If you see the oppression of the poor and the violent perversion of justice and righteousness in a province, do not be astonished at the matter, for the high official has one higher watching over him, and there are yet higher ones over them.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The layered bureaucratic hierarchy described reflects the administrative structure of Persian-period Judah or earlier Israelite monarchy, where provincial governors answered to satraps who answered to the king. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Some interpreters, reading within the framework of Second Temple cosmology, see in "higher ones over them" an oblique reference to the heavenly tier of governance — echoing Deut 32:8-9 and Ps 82, where divine beings preside over nations. The ambiguity may be intentional.

verses.flow: But the profit of the land is in all things; a king himself is subject to the field.

verses.flow: He who loves silver (keseph) will not be satisfied with silver, and he who loves abundance will have no fruit. This also is vanity (hebel).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Keseph" (H3701) means silver and by extension money or wealth generally. The structure of the verse uses the rhetorical pattern of unrequited love — loving something that cannot reciprocate satisfaction. THEOLOGICAL: The insatiability of wealth mirrors the broader vanity theme and anticipates NT warnings (1 Tim 6:6-10; Luke 12:15-21). True contentment (autarkeia in Paul) is the implied counter-virtue.

verses.flow: When goods increase, those who eat them increase; and what advantage has their owner except the seeing of them with his eyes?

verses.flow: There is a grievous evil (ra'ah) I have seen under the sun: riches kept by their owner to his harm.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ra'ah ra'ah" (grievous evil) is an intensifying construction — evil evil — indicating extreme severity. "Under the sun" (tachat ha-shemesh) is Ecclesiastes' characteristic phrase for terrestrial, mortal existence, occurring over two dozen times in the book. THEOLOGICAL: The observation that wealth hoarded for self-preservation ultimately destroys its owner is a theological irony: the very act of clinging to life produces the conditions of loss.

verses.flow: Behold, what I have seen to be good and fitting is to eat and to drink and to find enjoyment (tov) in all one's toil in which one toils under the sun, for the number of the days of his life that God has given him, for this is his portion (cheleq).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Cheleq" (H2506) means portion, share, or lot — the divinely assigned measure of one's earthly life. The word carries a covenantal resonance, as each tribe received its cheleq in the Promised Land. THEOLOGICAL: The repeated affirmation of eating, drinking, and finding tov (goodness/enjoyment) in one's toil is not hedonism but a creation-theology statement: embodied enjoyment within divinely given limits is itself a sacred gift. Compare 2:24; 3:12-13; 9:7-9.

verses.flow: a man to whom God gives wealth and possessions and honor, so that he lacks nothing of all he desires, yet God does not give him power to eat of it, but a stranger eats it. This is vanity (hebel) and a grievous affliction.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase translated "power to eat of it" renders the Hebrew root shalat (H7980), meaning dominion or mastery over something. The Preacher employs bitter irony: God grants every material good yet withholds the capacity to enjoy it — the enjoyment itself is a divine gift distinct from the possession. "Grievous affliction" renders ra'ah chalah (H7451 + H2470), literally "an evil sickness," intensifying the pathos. THEOLOGICAL: This verse anticipates the New Testament distinction between possessing and receiving with thanksgiving (1 Tim 6:17); true enjoyment is a grace, not a right.

verses.flow: If a man fathers a hundred children and lives many years, so that the days of his years are many, but his soul is not satisfied with good things, and he also has no burial, I say that a stillborn child is better off than he.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The stillborn child as a figure of tragic mercy recurs in Job 3:16, where Job wishes he had been like a hidden miscarriage. The Preacher's shocking reversal — that a life unlived is preferable to a long life devoid of satisfaction — undercuts ancient Near Eastern assumptions that longevity and progeny were unambiguous divine blessings. THEOLOGICAL: The absence of burial in Israelite thought is a profound dishonor (Jer 22:18-19), suggesting a life so empty it cannot command even the dignity of a proper ending. The passage forces reflection on what constitutes a truly human life before God.

verses.flow: All the toil of man is for his mouth, yet the appetite (nephesh) is not filled.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Appetite" here translates nephesh (H5315), the same term often rendered "soul." In this context it carries its concrete sense of the gullet or craving self — the whole desiring inner person. The Preacher observes that labor is oriented toward satisfying the nephesh, yet the nephesh remains perennially unsatisfied, an observation structurally parallel to Prov 27:20. THEOLOGICAL: Augustine's famous "our heart is restless until it rests in Thee" (Confessions 1.1) captures the theological trajectory this verse anticipates — creaturely appetite points beyond itself toward the Creator.

verses.flow: For what advantage has the wise man over the fool? And what does the poor man have who knows how to conduct himself before the living?
Better is the seeing of the eyes than the wandering of desire (nephesh). This also is vanity (hebel) and a striving after wind.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Seeing of the eyes" (mar'eh einayim, H4758 + H5869) refers to what is present and tangible; "wandering of desire" renders halak nephesh (lit. "going of the soul/appetite"), the restless movement of unfulfilled longing toward what is absent. The Preacher counsels a kind of chastened presence — engagement with the actual over obsession with the imagined. STRUCTURE: This verse functions as a pivot in the chapter, summarizing the critique of insatiable desire before the Preacher turns to cosmic and epistemological limits in vv. 10-12.

verses.flow: Whatever has come to be has already been named, and it is known what man is; and he is not able to contend with one mightier than he.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "One mightier than he" (taqqif mimmenu, H8623) almost certainly references God, whose sovereign naming of all things (echoing Gen 2) places man in a position of creature, not litigant. The verb "contend" (din, H1777) is legal language — man cannot bring a lawsuit against his Maker. This resonates with YHWH's own challenge to Job (Job 38:3; 40:2) and with the divine council's exclusive prerogative over cosmic order. THEOLOGICAL: The pre-naming of all things ("already been named") reflects the Hebraic understanding that to name is to define and determine essence — reality is already interpreted by the Creator before man arrives at it.

verses.flow: For who knows what is good for man while he lives the few days of his vain (hebel) life, which he passes like a shadow? For who can tell man what will be after him under the sun?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Vain life" renders yemei chayye hebelo (lit. "the days of the life of his vanity/breath"), an unusually dense genitive chain. "Like a shadow" (katstseil, H6738) evokes the fleeting, insubstantial quality of human existence — a theme shared with Ps 144:4 and Job 8:9. The double rhetorical question closing the chapter ("who knows... who can tell") is a studied epistemic humility. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter ends where Ecclesiastes consistently returns: the limits of human knowing and the opacity of futurity. This sets the stage for the fear of God as the only stable response (Eccl 12:13), a conclusion the Preacher circles throughout. TYPOLOGY: The question "what will be after him under the sun?" finds its answer only in the resurrection and eschatological disclosure promised in Christ (John 5:28-29; 1 Cor 15:20-28).

verses.flow: A name (shem) is better than precious ointment (shemen tov), and the day of death than the day of one's birth.
verses.note: HEBREW: The wordplay on "shem" (name, H8034) and "shemen" (ointment, H8081) is lost in translation — the Hebrew creates a sonic resonance between the two. In the ANE, a name represented one's entire identity and legacy; a good name outlasted expensive burial oils. TYPOLOGY: The Name above all names (Phil 2:9) surpasses all earthly anointing.

verses.flow: Better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting, for that is the end of all mankind, and the living will lay it to his heart.
Sorrow (ka'as) is better than laughter, for by the sadness of the face the heart is made glad.
The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth.
Better for a man to hear the rebuke of the wise than to hear the song of fools.
For as the crackling of thorns under the pot, so is the laughter of the fool — this also is vapor (hebel).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hebel" (H1892), the governing term of Ecclesiastes meaning vapor, breath, or vanity, here punctuates the poem on death and wisdom. The crackling thorns are vivid — they burn quickly and hotly but produce no lasting heat or light. THEOLOGICAL: Qohelet's realism refuses to romanticize the fool's joy; laughter without wisdom is as ephemeral as the vapor metaphor that frames the entire book.

verses.flow: Consider the work of God: who can make straight what he has made crooked?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Who can make straight what he has made crooked?" — this question is not pessimism but creational sovereignty. God's governance includes the bent and broken dimensions of existence; wisdom begins in receiving this rather than raging against it. TYPOLOGY: The Messiah alone can restore what is crooked (Isa 40:4, Luke 3:5); what Qohelet sees as humanly impossible, the gospel declares accomplished in Christ.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Hebrew phrase "gam et zeh le'ummat zeh" (one alongside the other) reveals Qohelet's understanding of providential hiddenness. God has structured reality so that no human being can fully predict or control outcomes, preserving divine freedom and human dependence. HISTORICAL: This reflects a Wisdom tradition that countered the mechanistic retribution theology sometimes read into Deuteronomy.

verses.flow: All of it I have seen in my days of vapor (hebel): there is a righteous man who perishes in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man who prolongs his life in his evildoing.
Be not overly righteous, and do not make yourself too wise — why should you destroy yourself?

It is good that you should take hold of this, and also from that do not let your hand rest; for the one who fears God (yir'at Elohim) shall go forth from them all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Yir'at Elohim" (fear of God) is the practical center of Wisdom theology in the Hebrew canon (Prov 1:7, Job 28:28). Here it is presented as the path through the tension of verses 16-17 — neither self-righteousness nor license, but reverent submission. The one who fears God "goes forth" from both extremes as from a trap.

Paul cites it in the spirit of his catena in Romans 3:10-23, building the case that all mankind stands in need of divine righteousness. TYPOLOGY: The sinless exception to this universal rule is the Messiah himself, who did what no earthly righteous man could do.

verses.flow: And I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets, and whose hands are fetters. He who is pleasing before God (Elohim) shall escape from her, but the sinner shall be captured by her.
Behold, this is what I have found, says the Preacher (Qohelet), adding one thing to another to find the sum,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qohelet" (H6953) derives from the root "qahal" meaning assembly or congregation — the Preacher is the one who gathers, whether people or wisdom sayings. The feminine grammatical form of the name in Hebrew ("amarah Qohelet," "says Qohelet," feminine verb) has puzzled commentators, possibly indicating a literary persona or a title rather than a personal name. STRUCTURE: This verse functions as a colophon within the chapter, a scribal marker signaling a conclusion to the preceding inquiry.

verses.flow: Behold, only this I have found: that God made man upright (yashar), but they have sought out many devices (chishbonot).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yashar" (H3477, upright, straight) echoes the creation theology of Genesis — man was made in moral and relational alignment. "Chishbonot" (H2810, devices, schemes, machinations) is the plural of the accounting term from v.25, creating an ironic contrast: humanity traded God's straight reckoning for its own crooked schemes. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is one of Ecclesiastes' clearest statements on the fall of mankind, affirming both original goodness and moral corruption without invoking Genesis explicitly. TYPOLOGY: The restoration of "yashar" in humanity is the messianic project — the Servant who is himself upright (Isa 53:11) imputes righteousness to the crooked.

verses.flow: Who is like the wise man? And who knows the interpretation (pesher) of a thing? A man's wisdom makes his face shine, and the hardness of his face is changed.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Interpretation" (pesher, פֵּשֶׁר, Strong's H6592) is rare in the Hebrew Bible but becomes prominent in the Dead Sea Scrolls, where the Qumran community used pesher-style commentary to interpret prophetic texts as fulfilled in their own day. The term carries the nuance of unlocking hidden meaning. The shining face of the wise recalls Moses (Exod 34:29-35), suggesting wisdom imparts a kind of radiant authority. TYPOLOGY: The one who perfectly "knows the interpretation of a thing" anticipates Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Col 2:3).

verses.flow: I say: Keep the command of the king, and because of the oath of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The double ground for obeying the king — royal command and divine oath — reflects the covenantal framework of Israelite kingship. The "oath of God" (shevuat Elohim, שְׁבוּעַת אֱלֹהִים) likely refers to the covenant oath sworn before God, grounding civil obedience in theological obligation. This anticipates the NT teaching on governing authorities as appointed by God (Rom 13:1-7).

verses.flow: Be not hasty; go from his presence. Do not take your stand in an evil thing, for he does whatever he pleases.
For the word of the king is supreme, and who may say to him, "What are you doing?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical question "Who may say to him, What are you doing?" is applied in Daniel to the divine King himself (Dan 4:35), and echoes the divine challenge to Job (Job 9:12). Qohelet uses royal absolutism as an analogy for the inscrutable sovereignty of God, preparing the reader for the climactic confession of verse 17.

verses.flow: Whoever keeps the commandment will know no evil thing, and the heart of the wise man knows both time and judgment (mishpat).

verses.flow: For he does not know what will be, for as it will be, who can tell him?

There is no man who has power over the ruach (ruach) to restrain the ruach, and there is no power over the day of death. There is no discharge in the battle, and wickedness will not deliver those who practice it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruach" (רוּחַ, Strong's H7307) carries the double meaning of "wind" and "spirit/breath," both of which are uncontrollable by human power. The fourfold enumeration of human powerlessness — over ruach, over death, in battle, and through wickedness — forms a structured argument about the limits of human authority. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "no discharge in battle" uses the military term mishlachat (מִשְׁלַחַת), indicating that death drafts every soldier with no furlough granted.

verses.flow: And so I saw the wicked buried; they came and went from the holy place, and they were forgotten in the city where they had done such things. This also is vapor (hebel).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Vapor" (hebel, הֶבֶל, Strong's H1892) is the book's governing term, denoting breath, mist, or vanity — that which is insubstantial and fleeting. The verse presents a bitter social observation: the wicked receive honorable burial and are celebrated in the very city where they practiced evil, then are forgotten. The textual tradition here is difficult; some manuscripts read "they were praised" instead of "forgotten," creating an ironic reversal. HISTORICAL: Honorable burial was of supreme importance in ANE culture; to receive proper burial rites was to be remembered and honored.

verses.flow: Because the sentence against an evil deed is not executed quickly, therefore the heart of the children of man is fully set in them to do evil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse diagnoses the root of moral disorder: delayed justice emboldens sin. The "sentence" (pitgam, פִּתְגָם, an Aramaic loanword, Strong's H6599) against evil not swiftly executed produces a culture of impunity. This insight anticipates the NT tension between divine patience and divine judgment (2 Pet 3:9), where God's delay is reframed as mercy rather than absence.

verses.flow: Though a sinner does evil a hundred times and prolongs his days, yet I know that it will be well with those who fear God, because they fear before him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verses 12-13 form the theological center of the chapter, asserting that the fear of God (yir'at Elohim, יִרְאַת אֱלֹהִים) is the determinative factor in human destiny, despite the confusing surface appearances of v. 14. Qohelet's "yet I know" (ki yodea ani, כִּי יוֹדֵעַ אָנִי) is a confession of faith that transcends empirical observation — a rare moment of positive certainty in the book.

verses.flow: But it will not be well with the wicked, neither will he prolong his days like a shadow, because he does not fear before God.

There is a vapor (hebel) that happens upon the earth: that there are righteous men to whom it happens according to the deeds of the wicked, and there are wicked men to whom it happens according to the deeds of the righteous. I said that this also is vapor (hebel).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse reaches the apex of the chapter's moral tension, articulating what theologians call the "retribution problem" — the righteous suffer as though wicked, and the wicked prosper as though righteous. Qohelet labels this hebel, not to deny justice, but to confess that observable reality under the sun is opaque and morally disorienting. This problem finds its resolution only in the cross, where the righteous One bore the fate of the wicked (2 Cor 5:21).

verses.flow: And I commended joy (simchah), for there is nothing better for man under the sun than to eat and to drink and to rejoice, for this will accompany him in his toil through the days of his life that God has given him under the sun.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Qohelet's commendation of joy (simchah, שִׂמְחָה, Strong's H8057) recurs as a refrain throughout the book (2:24; 3:12-13; 5:18; 9:7-9; 11:9). This is not hedonism but a theological affirmation that creaturely enjoyment is a gift of God. The phrase "this will accompany him" uses the verb lavah (לָוָה, "to accompany, cling"), suggesting joy is a companion in toil, not an escape from it. TYPOLOGY: The gift of joy in the midst of labor under fallen conditions anticipates the eschatological feast of the Kingdom (Rev 19:9).

verses.flow: then I saw all the work of God, that man is not able to find out the work that is done under the sun. However much man may toil in seeking, he will not find it out. Even though a wise man claims to know, he is not able to find it out.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with a triple repetition of man's inability to "find out" (matza, מָצָא) the work of God — a rhetorical intensification marking epistemic humility as the proper response to the inscrutability of providence. STRUCTURE: This verse forms a literary inclusio with 1:13-15, bracketing the entire investigative project of Qohelet. The "wise man" who "claims to know" but cannot find it out is a sobering qualification on human wisdom, driving the reader to revelation rather than speculation.

verses.flow: For all this I laid to my heart, even to examine all of this: that the righteous and the wise and their deeds are in the hand of God (Elohim). Whether love or hatred, man does not know; all is before them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The opening declaration that both the righteous and the wise are "in the hand of God (Elohim)" (beyadh ha-Elohim) is the Preacher's anchor amid radical uncertainty. The inscrutable nature of divine love and hatred echoes the covenantal tension of Malachi 1:2-3 and Paul's treatment of election in Romans 9:13. Qohelet does not deny providence but confesses its hiddenness from the human vantage point "under the sun."

verses.flow: This is an evil in all that is done under the sun: that one event befalls all. Also, the heart of the children of man is full of evil, and madness is in their heart while they live, and after that — to the dead.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "madness is in their heart" (holeloth bilebabam) reflects the Preacher's diagnosis of the human condition in its natural state — corrupted, disordered, and death-bound. This is one of the passages the Talmud (Shabbat 30b) found troubling, as it seems to equate the fates of righteous and wicked. Yet Qohelet's point is descriptive of temporal experience, not eternal destiny, consistent with the book's repeated call to fear God (12:13).

verses.flow: For whoever is joined to all the living has hope (betach), for a living dog is better than a dead lion.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "hope" here is betach (Strong's H983), meaning security, confidence, or trust. Some manuscripts and the LXX read yitaron ("advantage" or "gain"), which would yield "whoever is joined to all the living has an advantage." The dog-lion contrast is striking in ANE context where dogs were scavengers and lions symbols of royalty — yet the living scavenger surpasses the dead king.

verses.flow: Go, eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a merry heart, for already God has approved your works.
Let your garments be always white, and let not oil be lacking upon your head.

Enjoy life with the wife whom you love, all the days of your vain life that he has given you under the sun, all your days of vanity (hebel), for that is your portion in life and in your toil at which you toil under the sun.
verses.note: HEBREW: The key term "vanity" translates hebel (Strong's H1892), literally "breath" or "vapor" — the governing metaphor of Ecclesiastes. The command to enjoy life with one's wife echoes the blessing of Proverbs 5:18-19 and anticipates the Pauline principle that God "richly provides us with everything to enjoy" (1 Tim 6:17). TYPOLOGY: The enjoyment of life as a God-given "portion" points to the eschatological feast of the Kingdom, where the Messiah himself drinks wine anew with his own (Matt 26:29).

verses.flow: Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your might, for there is no work or thought or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The exhortation to full-hearted labor while life remains is grounded in the finality of Sheol — the Hebrew realm of the dead — where no activity or wisdom operates. This is not a denial of resurrection but a statement about the present age's urgency. The NT intensifies this logic: "Night is coming, when no one can work" (John 9:4). HEBREW: "Sheol" (she'ol, Strong's H7585) is the underworld, the destination of all the dead in OT anthropology, distinguished from the later developed concept of Gehenna.

verses.flow: Again I saw under the sun that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the men of understanding, nor favor to the men of skill; but time and chance (pega) befall them all.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word pega (Strong's H6294), here rendered "chance," literally means "occurrence," "encounter," or "happening." It is theologically significant that Qohelet does not use the word for random chance (mikreh) here but pega, implying an appointed encounter. THEOLOGICAL: The subversion of expected outcomes — the swift losing the race, the strong losing the battle — undermines human self-sufficiency and opens space for divine sovereignty. This passage resonates with Paul's declaration that God "chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong" (1 Cor 1:27).

verses.flow: And there was found in it a poor, wise man, and he by his wisdom delivered the city. Yet no one remembered that poor man.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The forgotten poor wise man who delivers a city is one of Ecclesiastes's most potent messianic shadows. He saves through wisdom rather than military power, and yet is not remembered by those he rescued. This anticipates the Servant of Isaiah 53 who "shall act wisely" (52:13) and yet "was despised and rejected by men" (53:3). The pattern of the despised deliverer is fulfilled in Christ, the one greater than Solomon (Matt 12:42), whose salvific wisdom the world forgets.

verses.flow: Then I said, "Better is wisdom than might, yet the wisdom of the poor man is despised, and his words are not heard."
The words of the wise heard in quiet are better than the shouting of a ruler among fools.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "heard in quiet" translates the Hebrew benachat nishma'im (lit. "in stillness are heard"), contrasting the receptive silence appropriate for wisdom with the empty noise of the powerful fool. THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates a recurring biblical epistemology: truth comes not through force or volume but through attentive stillness — echoed in Elijah's "still small voice" (1 Kgs 19:12) and the Sermon on the Mount's beatitude of the meek (Matt 5:5).

words.g: and good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Better is wisdom than weapons of war, but one sinner destroys much good.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The concluding proverb of the chapter returns to the paradox of wisdom's superiority and vulnerability. "One sinner destroys much good" (chote' echad ye'abbed tovah harbeh) demonstrates that evil operates asymmetrically — a single corrupting agent can undo the labor of many wise men. This is the logic behind the imputation of Adam's sin (Rom 5:15-19) inverted: as one man's trespass brought ruin, so one Man's righteous act brings restoration. STRUCTURE: This verse forms a thematic bracket with verse 1 — both acknowledge the unpredictability of outcomes for wisdom and righteousness in a fallen world "under the sun."

verses.flow: Dead flies make the oil of the perfumer give off a stench; a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Dead flies" (zevuvei mavet, H2071/H4194) — the construct chain literally reads "flies of death," an idiom for flies that cause death or putrification. The word for "perfumer's oil" (shemen rokeach) echoes the sacred anointing oil formula (Exod 30:25), heightening the contrast: a small corruption ruins something precious and set apart. THEOLOGICAL: The proverb encapsulates the Preacher's recurring theme that a little folly can undo great wisdom — a sober warning about moral integrity in leaders and sages alike.

verses.flow: The heart (lev) of the wise inclines to his right, but the heart of a fool to his left.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Heart" (lev, H3820) in Hebrew anthropology refers to the seat of intellect, will, and moral discernment — not merely emotion. "Right" and "left" carry connotations of good fortune and misfortune respectively in ancient Near Eastern thought, so the wise man's heart inclines toward what is beneficial and favorable. THEOLOGICAL: This verse has been read typologically in Jewish and Christian interpretation as reflecting the orientation of the whole person toward God (right) or away from Him (left), anticipating the sheep-and-goats imagery of Matt 25:33.

verses.flow: Even in the road, when the fool walks, his heart is lacking, and he says to everyone that he is a fool.
If the spirit (ruach) of the ruler rises against you, do not leave your place, for calmness will lay great offenses to rest.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Spirit" (ruach, H7307) here carries the sense of a ruler's emotional temperament or mood — the same word used for the Spirit of God and for wind, suggesting volatility. "Calmness" (marpeh, H4832) literally means "healing" or "a remedy," cognate with the verb rapha (to heal); the counsel is that a composed, healing demeanor disarms even grave offenses. HISTORICAL: This reflects wisdom instruction for royal courtiers common in ANE wisdom literature, including Egyptian instructions such as the Instruction of Amenemope.

verses.flow: There is an evil I have seen under the sun, like an error proceeding from before the ruler:
folly is set in many high places, and the rich sit in a low place.
I have seen servants upon horses, and princes walking on the ground like servants.

verses.flow: He who digs a pit will fall into it, and he who breaks through a wall, a serpent will bite him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The proverb of the pit and the serpent in the broken wall reflects practical agricultural and construction realities of the ancient Near East, where pits were dug for cisterns and storage and walls of loose stone harbored vipers. THEOLOGICAL: Beyond the practical, the verse functions as a theological law of retributive irony — the one who schemes harm against another is caught in his own device, a principle affirmed in Ps 7:15-16 and Prov 26:27. TYPOLOGY: The serpent in the wall may carry allusive resonance with the primordial serpent of Gen 3, suggesting that tampering with boundaries invites the ancient adversary.

verses.flow: He who quarries stones will be hurt by them, and he who splits logs will be endangered by them.
If the iron is blunt and one does not sharpen its edge, he must exert more strength; but wisdom (chokmah) is the advantage that brings success.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wisdom" (chokmah, H2451) is here contrasted with brute force; the word translated "advantage" or "profit" (yitron, H3504) is one of the Preacher's distinctive terms, appearing throughout Ecclesiastes as a measure of what truly yields lasting benefit. "Brings success" translates the hiphil of kashar (H3787), meaning to make prosper or to set right — wisdom is the factor that makes effort efficient.

verses.flow: If the serpent bites before it is charmed, then there is no advantage to the charmer.

verses.flow: The words of a wise man's mouth are gracious (chen), but the lips of a fool consume him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Gracious" (chen, H2580) — the noun from the root chanan, denoting favor, attractiveness, and that which draws others toward a person. It is the same root used of finding favor before God and men (Prov 3:4). The contrast between the gracious words of the wise and the self-consuming lips of the fool echoes Prov 10:11 and 18:7. THEOLOGICAL: The mouth as a vehicle of blessing or destruction is a pervasive biblical theme, reaching its fullest expression in Jas 3:1-12 and in Christ's teaching that out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks (Matt 12:34).

verses.flow: The beginning of the words of his mouth is folly, and the end of his talk is evil madness.

verses.flow: The toil of a fool wearies him, for he does not know the way to the city.
Woe to you, O land, when your king is a child (na'ar), and your princes feast in the morning!
verses.note: HEBREW: "Child" or "youth" (na'ar, H5288) does not necessarily denote chronological age but can indicate immaturity, inexperience, or servile status — the opposite of the noble dignity fitting a king. The feasting "in the morning" signifies self-indulgent disorder, as morning was the time for judicial administration and royal duty (2 Sam 15:2; Jer 21:12). HISTORICAL: Woe-oracles against irresponsible rulers are attested in the prophetic corpus (Isa 5:11-12; Amos 6:4-6) and reflect the ANE understanding that the king's virtue directly determines national welfare.

verses.flow: Blessed are you, O land, when your king is the son of free men (ben-chorim), and your princes feast at the proper time, in strength and not in drunkenness!
verses.note: HEBREW: "Son of free men" (ben-chorim, H1121/H2715) — chorim denotes those of noble birth or free civic standing, the opposite of a slave or a base-born ruler. The verse may be read as a veiled messianic ideal: the righteous king whose lineage is honorable and whose court feasts with discipline and strength. TYPOLOGY: The portrait of the ideal king here — righteous in origin, governing with restraint — foreshadows the Davidic Messiah whose kingdom is characterized by justice and joy (Isa 9:6-7; Luke 1:32-33).

verses.flow: Through sloth the rafters sink, and through the idleness of hands the house leaks.
Bread is made for laughter, and wine gladdens life, and money answers everything.
Even in your thoughts do not curse the king, and in your bedroom do not curse the rich, for a bird of the heavens will carry your voice, and a winged creature will tell the matter.
verses.note: HEBREW: "In your thoughts" (bemadda'echa, H4093) — madda is a rare term for knowledge, thought, or consciousness, found also in Dan 1:4; the phrase means even in the private chamber of your mind. The "bird of the heavens" carrying a voice is likely a proverbial expression, but DIVINE_COUNCIL: some interpreters read the heavenly bird as a figure for divine or angelic surveillance, consistent with the biblical motif that YHWH's council has access to hidden human speech (cf. Job 1:7; Zech 1:10-11). HISTORICAL: The proverb also parallels Egyptian wisdom warnings that the walls themselves report to Pharaoh, reflecting a widespread ANE conviction that rulers possess supernatural informants.

verses.flow: Cast your bread upon the face of the waters (mayim), for in the multitude of days you will find it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Cast your bread upon the face of the waters" — the verb shallach (H7971) implies a deliberate sending forth. "Bread" (lechem, H3899) may function here as a commercial or agricultural metaphor: grain shipped across trading waters, an act of bold generosity or investment whose return is uncertain but promised. The Talmud (Kohelet Rabbah) reads this as an exhortation to charitable giving, while ANE commercial parallels suggest maritime trade. Both senses cohere: act generously without certainty of return.

verses.flow: Give a portion to seven, and also to eight, for you do not know what evil will be upon the earth.

verses.flow: As you do not know the way of the wind (ruach), as bones in the womb of her who is with child, so you do not know the work of God (Elohim) who makes everything.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term ruach (H7307) carries the double sense of "wind" and "spirit," creating deliberate ambiguity — the wise reader hears both. Qohelet employs this ambiguity to elevate the epistemological point: as the path of the wind is inscrutable, so too the work of the divine Spirit in forming life in the womb is beyond human knowing. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the key epistemological hinge of chapter 11, grounding the call to bold action in human ignorance before the sovereign God (Elohim). God's creative governance (cf. Ps 139:13-16) exceeds all human calculation.

verses.flow: For if a man lives many years, in all of them let him rejoice, and let him remember the days of darkness (choshek), for they will be many; all that comes is vanity (hevel).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Vanity" renders hevel (H1892), literally "breath" or "vapor," the thematic keyword of Ecclesiastes appearing some 38 times. "Days of darkness" (choshek, H2822) anticipates death and the grave, not merely literal night. THEOLOGICAL: The juxtaposition of rejoicing and remembering death is characteristic of Qohelet's wisdom: genuine joy is not naive but held in full awareness of mortality. This dialectic prefigures the NT tension between present joy and eschatological sobriety (cf. 1 Cor 7:29-31).

verses.flow: Rejoice, O young man, in your youth, and let your heart make you glad in the days of your young manhood, and walk in the ways of your heart and in the sight of your eyes; but know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse has generated significant interpretive debate. The call to rejoice in youth followed immediately by the warning of divine judgment is not ironic retraction but covenantal realism — pleasure is affirmed as God's gift, but moral accountability frames all human experience. TYPOLOGY: The certainty of divine judgment (mishpat, H4941) for all deeds anticipates the eschatological reckoning central to the New Testament (cf. 2 Cor 5:10; Eccl 12:14), where the Messiah is appointed as judge of all.

verses.flow: And remove vexation (ka'as) from your heart, and put away evil from your flesh, for youth and the dawn of life are vanity (hevel).
verses.note: HEBREW: Ka'as (H3708) denotes not merely sadness but a turbulent, consuming vexation or grief — the kind that corrodes the heart. "Dawn of life" renders shacharut (H7839), a hapax legomenon possibly related to shachar ("dawn"), suggesting the fleeting brilliance of early youth. THEOLOGICAL: The double imperative — remove inner vexation, put away bodily evil — addresses both the inner and outer man, anticipating the summary conclusion of 12:13-14. The designation of youth as hevel is not cynicism but eschatological perspective: all temporal phases pass before the eternal God.

verses.flow: And remember your Creator in the days of your youth, before the evil days come and the years draw near of which you will say, "I have no delight (chephets) in them";
verses.note: HEBREW: "Remember" (zekhor, Strong's H2142) is a qal imperative, carrying covenantal weight — not merely cognitive recall but active, loyal orientation toward one's Creator. "Creator" is bore'ekha (from bara', H1254), the same root used in Gen 1:1, linking personal existence to cosmic origins. The plural form bore'ekha may echo the divine council or the plural of majesty, echoing "let us make man" (Gen 1:26). THEOLOGICAL: The entire book's argument resolves here: the antidote to the vanity of life under the sun is covenantal remembrance of YHWH from one's youth, before the body fails and the capacity for wholehearted devotion diminishes (Ps 71:5, 17).

verses.flow: in the day when the keepers of the house tremble, and the men of valor are bent, and the grinding women cease because they are few, and those who look through the windows grow dim;
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse initiates an extended allegory of aging, one of the most celebrated literary passages in the Hebrew Bible. The "keepers of the house" (shomerei ha-bayit) likely represent the arms and hands; "men of valor" (anshei he-chayil) the legs; "grinding women" the teeth; "those who look through the windows" (ha-ro'ot ba-arubboth) the eyes. The poet employs circumlocution (indirect description) consistent with Wisdom literature's preference for layered meaning. STRUCTURE: Verses 3–5 form a sustained poetic unit describing senescence through the metaphor of a deteriorating household and estate, a rhetorical technique with parallels in Egyptian wisdom literature.

verses.flow: they are also afraid of what is high, and terrors are in the way; and the almond tree blossoms, and the locust drags itself along, and desire (ha-abiyyonah) fails — for man goes to his eternal home, and the mourners go about the streets;
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "The almond tree blossoms" (yinets ha-shaqed) — the almond (shaqed) blooms white in late winter and is associated with old age and white hair. The same root underlies YHWH's wordplay in Jer 1:11–12 ("I am watching over my word"). "Desire fails" — ha-abiyyonah (the caper berry, Strong's H17) was used in antiquity as an appetite stimulant; its failure signals the extinction of appetite and vitality. THEOLOGICAL: "His eternal home" (beit olamo) is a rare phrase pointing to the realm of the dead, affirming that human existence does not terminate at death but transitions — the spirit's destination in v. 7 confirms this eschatological trajectory.

verses.flow: and the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit (ruach) returns to God who gave it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The spirit returns (tashuv) to God who gave it" — ruach (H7307) here denotes the animating breath-spirit given by God at creation (Gen 2:7). The verb shuv (return, H7725) implies the spirit's ultimate origin is God Himself, not the material world. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the climactic anthropological statement of Ecclesiastes: the dust-component of humanity returns to the earth (Gen 3:19), while the ruach returns to its divine source, implying a personal accountability before God. This anticipates the judgment of v. 14 and provides the theological ground for fearing God. TYPOLOGY: Christ's words on the cross — "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit" (Luke 23:46, citing Ps 31:5) — consciously echo this return of the human spirit to God, fulfilling the pattern as the Second Adam.

verses.flow: Vanity (hevel) of vanities, says the Preacher (Qohelet); all is vanity.
verses.note: HEBREW: Hevel (H1892), rendered "vanity," literally means "breath" or "vapor" — something insubstantial, fleeting, and without lasting grip. The superlative construction hevel havalim ("vanity of vanities") mirrors the Hebrew idiom for the absolute superlative (cf. "holy of holies," "song of songs"). STRUCTURE: This verse forms a literary inclusio with 1:2, bracketing the entire book with the same declaration and confirming that chapters 1–12 constitute a unified composition. The repetition at the book's close is not despair but the necessary premise that drives the reader to the resolution in vv. 13–14.

verses.flow: The words of the wise are like goads, and like nails firmly fixed are the collected sayings; they are given by one Shepherd.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Given by one Shepherd" (min-ro'eh echad) — the Shepherd here is widely understood as God Himself, the source of all true wisdom. This elevates the entire Wisdom tradition: Proverbs, Job, and Ecclesiastes are not merely human philosophy but divinely shepherded revelation. TYPOLOGY: Jesus identifies Himself as the one Good Shepherd (John 10:11, 14) and as Wisdom incarnate (Matt 12:42; 1 Cor 1:30), positioning Himself as the singular source from whom all the "goads" and "nails" of wisdom ultimately proceed.

verses.flow: The end of the matter, all having been heard: fear God and keep His commandments, for this is the whole duty of man (kol-ha-adam).
verses.note: HEBREW: Kol-ha-adam (literally "the whole of man" or "all of man") is the subject of the clause, not a predicate — "this is the whole of man" meaning the complete definition, purpose, and duty of human existence is encapsulated in fearing God and keeping His commandments. THEOLOGICAL: The "fear of God" (yir'at Elohim, H3374 + H430) throughout Wisdom literature is not servile terror but reverent, covenantal awe that orients all of life rightly (Prov 1:7; 9:10; Ps 111:10). This verse resolves the book's tension: life's apparent vanity does not render existence meaningless but drives the wise person back to its only solid foundation. TYPOLOGY: Jesus summarizes the Law in covenantal love of God and neighbor (Matt 22:37–40), and His perfect obedience constitutes the fulfillment of this whole-of-man duty on behalf of His people (Rom 5:19).

verses.flow: For God will bring every deed into judgment, with every hidden thing, whether good or evil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The book's final verse grounds all ethics and eschatology in divine judgment: every deed (kol-ma'aseh), including "every hidden thing" (kol-ne'elam), falls under God's scrutiny. This counters any hedonistic misreading of Ecclesiastes — the Teacher's encouragements to enjoy life are bounded by accountability. TYPOLOGY: Paul's declaration that "we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ" (2 Cor 5:10) and Jesus's warning that "nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest" (Luke 8:17) are direct developments of this verse. The Messiah who is the one Shepherd (v. 11) is also the eschatological Judge before whom all hidden things are disclosed.
